skill.name: enact-json-formatter
skill.description: Formats, validates, and prettifies JSON strings with configurable indentation.
skill.summary: Format JSON strings into readable, validated output.
skill.author: EnactProtocol

security_audit.summary: Most static findings are false positives: weak-cryptography matches occur in ordinary metadata words, and backtick detections are fenced usage examples. The manifest command interpolates user-controlled JSON into a command template, which creates a command-injection concern if the runtime uses shell parsing without safe escaping.

medium_findings.title: User Input Interpolated Into Command Template
medium_findings.description: The manifest command uses user-supplied json and indent values inside a command string. If the Enact runtime executes this string through a shell without robust argument escaping, crafted JSON could alter the executed command.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command template clearly contains user-controlled placeholders. The final risk depends on runtime escaping behavior, so this is a credible concern but not confirmed exploitation.

low_findings.title: Static Pattern Matches Were Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography and system-reconnaissance detections are ordinary schema and documentation text, not cryptographic code or host inspection. The backtick detections are fenced command and output examples, not executable skill logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are metadata descriptions, documentation fences, or example output. No cryptographic primitive, reconnaissance command, or executable backtick shell call appears there.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell-Sensitive Placeholder Expansion
dangerous_patterns.description: The command string includes placeholders for user-controlled values. Safe publication should depend on verified runtime escaping or a change to pass arguments without shell interpretation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The placeholder expansion is visible in the manifest. The exact exploitability cannot be confirmed without Enact execution semantics.

content.value_statement: Messy JSON is hard to inspect, compare, and share. This skill validates JSON strings and returns readable formatting with a chosen indentation level.
actual_capabilities: Parses a JSON string and reports whether it is valid.
Returns a prettified JSON string when parsing succeeds.
Returns structured error details when parsing fails.
Runs as a small Node.js command inside the declared container.
limitations: It does not repair invalid JSON automatically.
It accepts JSON as a command argument, so very large payloads may fail.
It does not sort keys or apply schema validation.
Safe command argument handling depends on the Enact runtime.

use_cases.title: Inspect API Responses
use_cases.description: Format compact API response bodies before reviewing fields or sharing output in an issue.

use_cases.title: Clean Configuration Snippets
use_cases.description: Validate and reformat pasted configuration fragments before adding them to documentation.

use_cases.title: Prepare Debug Evidence
use_cases.description: Convert minified JSON into readable output for support tickets and incident notes.

prompt_templates.title: Format a Small JSON String
prompt_templates.prompt: Format this JSON with two spaces of indentation and tell me if it is valid.

prompt_templates.title: Validate Before Sharing
prompt_templates.prompt: Validate this JSON and return a readable version if it parses successfully.

prompt_templates.title: Use Custom Indentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Format this JSON with four spaces of indentation and preserve the original field values.

prompt_templates.title: Triage Invalid Input
prompt_templates.prompt: Run the formatter on this JSON, report the parse error, and explain the likely syntax problem.

output_examples.input: A compact customer record with nested fields.
output_examples.output: The skill returns valid status and a readable, indented representation of the same data.

output_examples.input: A pasted payload with a missing comma.
output_examples.output: The skill returns invalid status and an error message from the parser.

output_examples.input: A minified settings object with four-space indentation requested.
output_examples.output: The skill returns the same settings in a multi-line layout using four-space indentation.

best_practices: Use it with moderate-size JSON strings that fit safely in command arguments.
Review parse errors before assuming the source data is corrupted.
Choose indentation that matches the project or document style.
anti_patterns: Do not use it as a schema validator for business rules.
Do not pass secrets unless the execution environment is trusted.
Do not rely on it to repair malformed JSON automatically.

faq.question: Does this skill validate JSON?
faq.answer: Yes. It parses the input and returns whether the JSON is valid.

faq.question: Can it change indentation?
faq.answer: Yes. Provide an indentation value to control the number of spaces.

faq.question: Does it sort object keys?
faq.answer: No. It preserves the parsed object structure and uses standard formatting.

faq.question: Can it fix invalid JSON?
faq.answer: No. It reports parser errors but does not rewrite invalid input.

faq.question: Is it suitable for large files?
faq.answer: It is best for strings that fit comfortably in command arguments.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
